title: Reinforcement Learning Fundamentals
description: Master the core concepts of reinforcement learning through interactive examples and assessments.

nodes.title: What is Reinforcement Learning?
nodes.content: Reinforcement Learning (RL) is a machine learning approach where an agent learns to make decisions by interacting with an environment. The agent observes states (current situation), takes actions (decisions), receives rewards or penalties (feedback), and aims to maximize cumulative reward over time. Unlike supervised learning where labeled data guides learning, RL agents learn through trial and error—discovering which actions lead to better outcomes. This agent-environment interaction creates a feedback loop: the agent acts, the environment responds with rewards, and the agent uses this signal to improve future decisions. RL powers game-playing AIs like AlphaGo, robot control systems that learn manipulation, and autonomous systems that optimize complex behaviors.

nodes.title: Grid World: A Simple MDP
nodes.content:
Let's explore a Markov Decision Process (MDP)—the mathematical framework for RL problems. Imagine a robot on a 3×3 grid that must reach the goal (G) while avoiding obstacles (X). The robot starts at S.

Grid:
S . . 
. X . 
. . G

The environment defines:
• States: Each grid cell is a state (e.g., "robot at position (0,0)")
• Actions: The robot can move up, down, left, right
• Rewards: Each move costs -1 (encourages efficiency), reaching G grants +10 (the goal)
• Transitions: Moving right from S leads deterministically to the right cell

The robot learns that going right, then down, then right reaches G in 3 steps with total reward: 3×(-1) + 10 = 7. An MDP has all these elements: a set of states S, actions A, immediate rewards R, and transition dynamics P(s'|s,a). This structure lets us formally reason about agent learning.

nodes.title: How Agents Learn: The Trial-and-Error Cycle
nodes.content:
Now let's see how an agent actually learns in an MDP. Our robot starts with a random policy—it picks actions without any strategy. Here's what happens:

Episode 1 (random exploration):
• At S, robot randomly moves right: gets -1 reward, now at cell (0,1)
• From (0,1), randomly moves down: gets -1 reward, now at cell (1,1)
• At (1,1) there's an obstacle! Hits it, gets -1 reward, stays at (1,1)
• Randomly moves right: gets -1 reward, now at (1,2)
• From (1,2), randomly moves down: gets +10 for reaching G!
• Episode return: -1 + (-1) + (-1) + (-1) + 10 = 6

Episode 2-10: The robot tries many different random paths. Some crash into obstacles (low returns), others reach G efficiently (high returns).

Learning: After many episodes, the robot notices: "Paths that go right and down reach the goal and get +10." Its policy gradually shifts toward these successful actions. When in state S, it learns to prefer moving right. When at (1,1) and avoided the obstacle, it learns down is dangerous from there. This accumulated experience shapes the policy, replacing random choices with learned preferences. This is reinforcement learning: the reward signal guides the agent toward better decisions through repeated experience.

nodes.title: RL Fundamentals Check
nodes.content: Test your understanding of core reinforcement learning concepts.

questions.question: In reinforcement learning, what does an agent learn by interacting with the environment?
feedback: A) No, RL uses unlabeled feedback through rewards.
B) Correct! The agent learns a policy mapping states to actions.
C) No, RL is unsupervised learning via rewards.
D) No, the agent discovers policies through experience.
questions.explanation: The core goal of RL is to learn a policy—a strategy that tells the agent what action to take in each state to maximize reward.

questions.question: In the Grid World example, if each move costs -1 and reaching the goal gives +10, what is the total reward for the optimal 3-step path?
feedback: A) No, we must account for movement cost.
B) Correct! 3 moves × (-1) + 10 = 7.
C) No, the final reward is +10, not -3.
D) No, recalculate: -1 - 1 - 1 + 10 = 7.
questions.explanation: The optimal path costs -3 in movement penalties but gains +10 for reaching the goal, totaling +7 reward.

nodes.title: Policy and Value Functions
nodes.content: A policy π (pi) is the core of what an agent learns—a rule or strategy that maps states to actions. There are two main types: (1) Deterministic policies always choose one action per state (e.g., π(s) = "move right"). (2) Stochastic policies assign probabilities to actions (e.g., 70% chance move right, 20% up, 10% down). A value function V(s) estimates the long-term reward you can expect from being in state s. It answers: "If I'm here and follow my current policy, how much total discounted reward will I get?" The Bellman equation connects policy and value: V(s) = r + γV(s'), where r is the immediate reward from the current state, γ (gamma) is a discount factor (typically 0.9-0.99) that weights future rewards lower than immediate rewards, and V(s') is the estimated value of the next state. This recursive relationship is powerful—it lets agents estimate long-term consequences of being in a state without simulating the entire future. By improving policies to lead toward high-value states, agents learn increasingly better decision strategies. Value functions are foundational to Q-learning, SARSA, policy gradients, and actor-critic methods—virtually all modern RL algorithms use them.

nodes.title: Bellman Equation in Action
nodes.content:
Let's compute values using the Bellman equation in a concrete scenario. Consider our robot at state S with three possible next moves:

Scenario 1: Move right
• Immediate reward r = -1 (movement cost)
• Next state (0,1) has estimated value V(0,1) = 8 (we've learned this state usually leads to goal)
• Using γ = 0.9: V_right(S) = -1 + 0.9 × 8 = -1 + 7.2 = 6.2

Scenario 2: Move down  
• Immediate reward r = -1
• Next state (1,0) has value V(1,0) = 2 (dead end)
• V_down(S) = -1 + 0.9 × 2 = -1 + 1.8 = 0.8

Scenario 3: Move left
• Immediate reward r = -1  
• Next state would be off-grid, bad (wall): V(wall) = -10
• V_left(S) = -1 + 0.9 × (-10) = -1 - 9 = -10

Conclusion: Moving right has the highest value (6.2), so a learned policy would prefer right from S. The Bellman equation lets agents evaluate and compare actions by looking one step ahead, accounting for immediate cost and discounted future value. Over many episodes, agents learn accurate value estimates V(s) for each state, and policies that navigate toward high-value states naturally emerge—this is value-driven learning.

nodes.title: Policy and Value Functions Assessment
nodes.content: Verify your understanding of how policies and value functions drive learning.

questions.question: What does a value function V(s) represent in reinforcement learning?
options: A) The immediate reward of taking an action
B) The long-term discounted reward expected from a state
C) The optimal action to take in a state
D) The probability of reaching the goal state
feedback: A) No, that's the immediate reward r, not V(s).
B) Correct! V(s) estimates cumulative discounted future reward from state s.
C) No, that's what a policy does.
D) No, value functions estimate expected reward, not probability.
questions.explanation: A value function predicts the total future reward from a given state, accounting for the discount factor.

questions.question: Using Bellman: V(S) = -1 + 0.8 × V(S') where V(S') = 10. What is V(S)?
feedback: B) No, check the math again.
D) No, the discount factor reduces the next state's value contribution.
questions.explanation: V(S) = -1 + 0.8 × 10 = -1 + 8 = 7. The discount factor weights future reward lower than immediate reward.

nodes.title: Practice: Reinforce Core Concepts
nodes.content: Strengthen your understanding with additional worked problems and calculations. Review these scenarios to solidify your grasp of policies, values, and the Bellman equation before moving on.

questions.question: An agent in state S can go left (immediate reward 1, next value 2) or right (immediate reward 5, next value 0) with γ=0.9. Which is better?
options: D) Cannot determine without more information
feedback: A) This yields lower value.
B) Correct! Right gives 5, which exceeds left's 2.8.
C) No, they have different values.
D) No, we have sufficient information to compare.
questions.explanation: Right action yields higher total value (5 > 2.8), so the optimal policy chooses right from this state.

questions.question: In the Bellman equation V(s) = r + γV(s'), what does the discount factor γ do?
options: A) Scales the magnitude of rewards
B) Reduces the weight of future value relative to immediate reward
feedback: A) No, r is the immediate reward.
B) Correct! γ controls how much future reward matters relative to immediate reward.
C) No, policy probabilities are π(a|s).
D) No, transition probability is P(s'|s,a).
questions.explanation: The discount factor γ (typically 0.9-0.99) makes future rewards less valuable than immediate rewards, encouraging timely action.

questions.question: An agent is in state S with V(S) = 4. It takes an action with immediate reward 2 and arrives at state S' with V(S') = 5. If γ = 0.8, what is the return for this single step?
feedback: A) No, you need to include the discounted future value.
B) No, that's just the next state's value.
C) Incorrect calculation.
D) Correct! Return = immediate reward + discounted next value = 2 + 0.8 × 5 = 6.
questions.explanation: In the Bellman framework, the value of an action is the immediate reward plus the discounted value of the resulting state.

nodes.title: What's Next?
nodes.content: Excellent work! You've successfully mastered the fundamentals of reinforcement learning—you understand what RL is, how agents learn through interaction, the concept of MDPs, policies, and basic value functions. At this point, you have two meaningful paths: You can dive deeper into the mathematical and algorithmic foundations of RL by exploring advanced topics (value-based algorithms like Q-learning and policy gradient methods), or you can complete the course with the solid foundational knowledge you've gained. Both are valid—choose based on your learning goals and available time.

nodes.title: Course Complete!
nodes.content: Congratulations! You've successfully learned the fundamentals of reinforcement learning. You now understand the agent-environment interaction model, Markov Decision Processes, how agents learn policies through trial and error, and the role of value functions in guiding learning. These core concepts form the foundation for advanced algorithms like Q-learning, policy gradients, and actor-critic methods. Use this knowledge to build intelligent systems that learn from interaction and optimize decision-making in complex environments. Whether you complete this course here or continue to explore advanced topics, you've built a solid understanding of how machines learn to make decisions through reward-driven interaction.
